name: CHEMISTRY (Theory)
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/1/1 (set-1)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them.

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - Section A, B and C.
  3. Section - A Q. Nos. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section - B Q. Nos. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section - C Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is NOT allowed.

SECTION A
1.

Define rate of reaction. Write two factors that affect the rate of reaction.

 2
2.

Arrange the following compounds as directed: (any Two)

  1. In decreasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution:
    C2H5NH2, (C2H5)2NH, (C2H5)3N
  2. In increasing order of solubility in water:
    (C2H5)2NH, C2H5NH2, C6H5NH2
  3. In decreasing order of their pKb values:
    C6H5NH2, C2H5NH2, NH3
 2
3.

Write the Nernst equation for the following cell reaction:
Zn(s) + Cu2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + Cu(s)
How will the Ecell be affected when concentration of

  1. Cu2+ ions is increased and
  2. Zn2+ ions is increased?
 2

SECTION - B
4.

The following ions of the 3d-transition series are given:
Ti4+, V3+, Cr3+, Mn3+
(Atomic number: Ti = 22, V = 23, Cr = 24, Mn = 25)
Identify the ion which is

  1. most stable in aqueous solution.
  2. a strong oxidizing agent.
  3. colourless in aqueous solution.

Give suitable reason in each.

 3
5.(i)
  1. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d4 ion if {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex}.
  2. Using valence bond theory, predict the hybridization and magnetic character of [Ni(CN)4]2-.
    (Atomic number of Ni = 28)
  3. Write the formula of the following complex using IUPAC norms:
    Dichloridobis (ethane-1,2-diamine) cobalt (III)
 3

OR
(ii)

When a co-ordination compound NiCl2{tex}\cdot{/tex}6H2O mixed with AgNO3, 2 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write

  1. The structural formula of the complex.
  2. Secondary valency of Ni in the complex.
  3. IUPAC name of the complex.
 3
6.

A first order reaction is 50% complete in 40 minutes. Calculate the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction. [Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 10 = 1]

 3
7.(i)

Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case:

  1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis.
  2. Carbylamine reaction.
  3. Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction.
 3

OR
(ii)

Write the structures of A, B and C in the following reactions:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{Cl}\xrightarrow{KCN}A\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow[{0^\circ\mathrm C}]{{\mathrm{HNO}}_2}\mathrm C{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[\mathrm\Delta]{{\mathrm{NH}}_3}A\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(a\operatorname{) }{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}}\mathrm C{/tex}
 3
8.(i)

Account for the following:

  1. Transition elements have higher enthalpies of atomisation.
  2. Separation of a mixture of Lanthanoid elements is difficult.
  3. The {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^0{/tex} value for copper is positive.
 3

OR
(ii)

Define transition elements. Which of the d-block elements may not be regarded as the transition elements? Why transition metals generally form coloured compounds?

 3
9.

An organic compound X with the molecular formula C5H10O forms 2,4-DNP derivative, does not reduce Tollens' reagent but gives positive iodoform test on heating with I2 in the presence of NaOH. Compound X gives ethanoic acid and propanoic acid on vigorous oxidation. Write the

  1. Structure of the compound X.
  2. Structure of the product obtained when compound X reacts with 2,4-DNP reagent.
  3. Structures of the products obtained when compound X is heated with I2 in the presence of NaOH.
 3
10.(i)

Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption.

 3

OR
(ii)

Define the following terms with a suitable example in each:

  1. Lyophilic sol
  2. Macromolecular colloid
  3. Coagulation
 3
11.
  1. The standard Gibbs energy {tex}\left(\Delta \mathrm{rG}^{\circ}\right){/tex} for the following cell reaction is -300 kJ mol-1:
    Zn(s) + 2Ag+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s)
    Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} for the reaction. (Given: 1F = 96500 mol-1)
  2. Calculate {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^o{/tex} for MgCl2 if {tex}\lambda^o{/tex} values for Mg2+ ion and Cl- ion are 106 S cm2mol-1 and 76.3 S cm2mol-1 respectively.
 3

SECTION - C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

Aldehydes, ketones and carboxylic acids are some of the important classes of organic compounds containing carbonyl group. These are highly polar molecules due to higher electro-negativity of oxygen relative to carbon in the carbonyl group. Aldehydes are prepared by dehydrogenation or controlled oxidation of primary alcohols and controlled reduction of acyl halides. Ketones are prepared by oxidation of secondary alcohols and hydration of alkynes.
Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition reactions onto the carbonyl group but carboxylic acid does not undergo nucleophilic addition reaction. The alpha ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) - hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic. Therefore aldehydes and ketones having at least one {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen undergo Aldol condensation.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised by mild oxidising agents such as Tollens' reagent and Fehling's reagent. Carboxylic acids are prepared by the oxidation of primary alcohols, aldehydes and by hydrolysis of nitriles. Aromatic carboxylic acids are prepared by side-chain oxidation of alkyl benzenes. Carboxylic acids are considerably more acidic than alcohols and most of simple phenols.
(i)

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction.:
CH3COCH3, CH3CHO, HCHO, C6H5COCH3

 1
(ii)

Give a simple chemical test to distinguish between Ethanal and Propanone.

 1
(iii)

Why carboxylic acid does not give nucleophilic addition reactions like aldehydes and ketones?

 1
(iv)
  1. Why {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature?
  2. Write the products in the following:
 2

OR

Write the major products of the following reactions:

 2

answer_key_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/1/1 (set-1)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them.

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - Section A, B and C.
  3. Section - A Q. Nos. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section - B Q. Nos. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section - C Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is NOT allowed.

SECTION : A
1.

Define rate of reaction. Write two factors that affect the rate of reaction.

Solution:

Rate of chemical reaction is equal to change in concentration of reactant and product in unit time.
Rate of reaction = {tex}\frac{\text{Change in concentration of reactant and product}}{\text{ Time interval}}{/tex}
Two factors:

  1. Concentration of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the concentration of reactants.
  2. Temperature of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the temperature. For 410o C rise in temperature rate of reaction generally becomes double.
 2
2.

Arrange the following compounds as directed: (any Two)

2
  1. In decreasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution:
    C2H5NH2, (C2H5)2NH, (C2H5)3N
  2. In increasing order of solubility in water:
    (C2H5)2NH, C2H5NH2, C6H5NH2
  3. In decreasing order of their pKb values:
    C6H5NH2, C2H5NH2, NH3
Solution:
  1. In aqueous solution, basic strength decreases in the order. (C2H5)NH > (C2H5)3N > C2H5NH2
  2. nhIn increasing order of solubility in water:
    (C2H5)NH2> (C2H5)2NH> C6H5NH2
  3. In decreasing order of their pKb values:
    NH3 > C2H5NH2 > C6H5NH2
 2
3.

Write the Nernst equation for the following cell reaction:
Zn(s) + Cu2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + Cu(s)
How will the Ecell be affected when concentration of

  1. Cu2+ ions is increased and
  2. Zn2+ ions is increased?
Solution:

Zn + Cu2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ + Cu
Ecell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}- {tex}\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}

  1. Ecell increases when concentration of Cu2+ ions increases.
  2. Ecell decreases when concentration of Zn2+ ions increases.
 2

SECTION : B
4.

The following ions of the 3d-transition series are given:
Ti4+, V3+, Cr3+, Mn3+
(Atomic number: Ti = 22, V = 23, Cr = 24, Mn = 25)
Identify the ion which is

  1. most stable in aqueous solution.
  2. a strong oxidizing agent.
  3. colourless in aqueous solution.

Give suitable reason in each.

Solution:
  1. Ti4+ = 3d, V3+ = 3d3, Mn3+ = 3d4, Cr3+ = 3d3 out of these ions Cr3+ is most stable due to crystal feild spliting concept. In Cr3+, t2g in half filled, so extra stable.
  2. Mn3+ is strong oxidising agent because Mn3+ can easily be converted into Mn2+ having d5 configuration which is a stable half-filled configuration.
  3. Ti4+ is colourless because it is having no unpaired e- for excitation to higher energy level and it is colourless.
 3
5.(i)
  1. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d4 ion if {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex}.
  2. Using valence bond theory, predict the hybridization and magnetic character of [Ni(CN)4]2-.
    (Atomic number of Ni = 28)
  3. Write the formula of the following complex using IUPAC norms:
    Dichloridobis (ethane-1,2-diamine) cobalt (III)
Solution:
  1. On the basis of crystal field theory, for a d4 ion, if {tex}\Delta_0{/tex}< P, then the complex is a high spin complex formed by the association of weak field ligands with the metal ion. As a result, the fourth electron enters one of the eg orbitals, thereby, exhibiting the electronic configuration {tex}t_{2 g}^3 e_g^1{/tex}.
  2. Ni atom (z = 28)
    E.C of Ni. = [Ar]4s23d8
    E.C. of Ni2+ = [Ar]4s03d8

    It is diamagnetic, due to the absence of unpaired electrons.
    Shape - square planar.
  3. [CoCl2(en)2]
 3

OR
(ii)

When a co-ordination compound NiCl2{tex}\cdot{/tex}6H2O mixed with AgNO3, 2 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write

  1. The structural formula of the complex.
  2. Secondary valency of Ni in the complex.
  3. IUPAC name of the complex.
Solution:
  1. [Ni(H2O)6]Cl2]
  2. Secondary valency = 6
  3. Hexaaquanickel (II) chloride
 3
6.

A first order reaction is 50% complete in 40 minutes. Calculate the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction. [Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 10 = 1]

Solution:

{tex}\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{40 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}}{/tex}
90% completion
t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} log {tex}\frac {\left[\mathrm{R}_0\right]} {[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2.303} { 0.693}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 40 {tex}\times{/tex} log{tex}\frac{100}{10}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2.303}{0.693}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 132.9 min

 3
7.(i)

Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case:

  1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis.
  2. Carbylamine reaction.
  3. Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction.
Solution:
  1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: is used for the preparation of aliphatic primary amines. In this reaction, phthalimide is first of all treated with ethanolic KOH to form potassium phthalimide. Potassium phthalimide on treatment with alkyl halide gives N-alkyl phthalimide, which on hydrolysis with dilute hydrochloric acid gives a primary amine as the product.
  2. Carbylamine reaction: is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test.
    {tex}\underset{Primary\;amine}{R-NH_2}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex}{tex}\underset{Carbylamine}{R-NC}{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex}
    For example,
    {tex}\underset{Methnamine}{CH_3-NH_2}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex}{tex}\underset{or\;methyl\;isocyanide}{\underset{Methyl\;Carbylamine}{CH_3-NC}}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex}
  3. Hoffmann bromamide reaction:
    When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. This degradation reaction is known as Hoffmann bromamide reaction. This reaction involves the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon atom of the amide to the nitrogen atom.

    For example,
 3

OR
(ii)

Write the structures of A, B and C in the following reactions:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{Cl}\xrightarrow{KCN}A\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow[{0^\circ\mathrm C}]{{\mathrm{HNO}}_2}\mathrm C{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[\mathrm\Delta]{{\mathrm{NH}}_3}A\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(a\operatorname{) }{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}}\mathrm C{/tex}
Solution:


3
8.(i)

Account for the following:

  1. Transition elements have higher enthalpies of atomisation.
  2. Separation of a mixture of Lanthanoid elements is difficult.
  3. The {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^0{/tex} value for copper is positive.
Solution:
  1. Because of greater number of unpaired electrons which cause strong metallic bonding and strong interatomic interaction forces.
  2. Because of lanthanoid contraction, similar atomic or ionic radii and similar properties.
  3. Because of high {tex}\Delta a H^{\circ}{/tex} and low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} H^{\circ}{/tex}
 3

OR
(ii)

Define transition elements. Which of the d-block elements may not be regarded as the transition elements? Why transition metals generally form coloured compounds?

Solution:
  1. Transition elements are defined as an element having a d-subshell that is partially filled with electrons, or an element that has the ability to form stable cations with an incompletely filled d-orbital.
    e.g., Cr has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d54s1, partially filled d-subshell, so it is transition element.
  2. Zn, Cd and Hg cannot be classified as transition elements as they have completely filled d-subshell, e.g., Zn has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d1042, has completely filled d-subshell, so cannot be classified as transition element.
  3. Transition elements generally form coloured compounds on account of d-d transitions, due to partial absorption of visible light occur and d-d transition of electron occurs in d-orbital, which causes the colour of the compound.
 3
9.

An organic compound X with the molecular formula C5H10O forms 2,4-DNP derivative, does not reduce Tollens' reagent but gives positive iodoform test on heating with I2 in the presence of NaOH. Compound X gives ethanoic acid and propanoic acid on vigorous oxidation. Write the

  1. Structure of the compound X.
  2. Structure of the product obtained when compound X reacts with 2,4-DNP reagent.
  3. Structures of the products obtained when compound X is heated with I2 in the presence of NaOH.
Solution:
  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\mathrm O}{\underset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex}

  3. CH3-CH2CH2COONa + CHI3
 3
10.(i)

Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption.

Solution:

Chapter Delete from the Textbook.

 3

OR
(ii)

Define the following terms with a suitable example in each:

  1. Lyophilic sol
  2. Macromolecular colloid
  3. Coagulation
Solution:

Chapter Deleted from the Textbook.

 3
11.
  1. The standard Gibbs energy {tex}\left(\Delta \mathrm{rG}^{\circ}\right){/tex} for the following cell reaction is -300 kJ mol-1:
    Zn(s) + 2Ag+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s)
    Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} for the reaction. (Given: 1F = 96500 mol-1)
  2. Calculate {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^o{/tex} for MgCl2 if {tex}\lambda^o{/tex} values for Mg2+ ion and Cl- ion are 106 S cm2mol-1 and 76.3 S cm2mol-1 respectively.
Solution:
  1. Zn(s) + 2Ag+(aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s)
    The number of moles of electrons transferred (n) is 2, as two moles of electrons are involved in the reduction of two moles of {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{\wedge}{/tex} + ions.
    Now, you are given {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -300{tex}\frac{kJ}{mol}{/tex}, but you need to convert it to joules (J) by multiplying by 1000 (since 1 kJ = 1000 J):
    {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -300{tex}\frac{kJ}{mol}{/tex} {tex}\times {/tex}1000 {tex}\frac{J}{kJ}{/tex} = -300,000{tex}\frac{J}{mol}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}{/tex}
    Now, plug in the values into the equation and solve for Eo:
    -300,000 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{mol}}{/tex}= -(2 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{~C}}{\mathrm{mol}}{/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} Eo
    Given the cell reaction:
    Let's calculate Eo:
    Eo = {tex}\frac{-300,000 \frac{\mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{mol}}}{-(2 \mathrm{~mol} \times 96500 \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{mol}})}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 3.11 V
    So, the standard cell potential Eo for the given cell reaction is approximately 3.11 V.
  2. MgCl2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mg2+ + 2Cl-
    {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{MgCl}_2\right){/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right){/tex} + {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{MgCl}_2\right){/tex} = 106S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 76.3S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex} = 258.6S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex}
    The limiting molar conductivity of MgCl2 is 258.6 S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex}
 3

SECTION : C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

Aldehydes, ketones and carboxylic acids are some of the important classes of organic compounds containing carbonyl group. These are highly polar molecules due to higher electro-negativity of oxygen relative to carbon in the carbonyl group. Aldehydes are prepared by dehydrogenation or controlled oxidation of primary alcohols and controlled reduction of acyl halides. Ketones are prepared by oxidation of secondary alcohols and hydration of alkynes.
Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition reactions onto the carbonyl group but carboxylic acid does not undergo nucleophilic addition reaction. The alpha ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) - hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic. Therefore aldehydes and ketones having at least one {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen undergo Aldol condensation.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised by mild oxidising agents such as Tollens' reagent and Fehling's reagent. Carboxylic acids are prepared by the oxidation of primary alcohols, aldehydes and by hydrolysis of nitriles. Aromatic carboxylic acids are prepared by side-chain oxidation of alkyl benzenes. Carboxylic acids are considerably more acidic than alcohols and most of simple phenols.

 5
(i)

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction.:
CH3COCH3, CH3CHO, HCHO, C6H5COCH3

Solution:

C6H5COCH3 < CH3COCH3 < CH3CHO < HCHO

 1
(ii)

Give a simple chemical test to distinguish between Ethanal and Propanone.

Solution:

On heating with tollens' reagent, ethanal forms silver mirror whereas propanone does not.

 1
(iii)

Why carboxylic acid does not give nucleophilic addition reactions like aldehydes and ketones?

Solution:

Because of resonance by -OH of COOH which reduces the electrophilicity of carboxyl carbon / because of resonance in COOH group due to which carbon loses its carbonyl nature.

 1
(iv)
  1. Why {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature?
  2. Write the products in the following:
Solution:
  1. Due to strong electron withdrawing effect of the carbonyl group and resonance stabilization of the conjugate base.

 2

OR

Write the major products of the following reactions:

Solution:


2
answer_key_text: Annual Exam - 2022 Code: 56/1/1 (set-1) Please check that this question paper contains 12 printed pages. Q.P. Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate. Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions. Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period. CHEMISTRY (Theory) Time allowed:2 hours Maximum Marks:35 General Instructions : Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory. This question paper is divided into three Sections - Section A , B and C . Section - A Q. Nos. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions carrying 2 marks each. Section - B Q. Nos. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions carrying 3 marks each. Section - C Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks. Use of log tables and calculators is NOT allowed. SECTION : A 1. Define rate of reaction. Write two factors that affect the rate of reaction. Solution: Rate of chemical reaction is equal to change in concentration of reactant and product in unit time. Rate of reaction = {tex}\frac{\text{Change in concentration of reactant and product}}{\text{ Time interval}}{/tex} Two factors: Concentration of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the concentration of reactants. Temperature of reactants: Rate of reaction increases on increasing the temperature. For 410 o C rise in temperature rate of reaction generally becomes double. 2 2. Arrange the following compounds as directed: (any Two ) 2 In decreasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution: C 2 H 5 NH 2 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N In increasing order of solubility in water: (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, C 2 H 5 NH 2 , C 6 H 5 NH 2 In decreasing order of their pKb values: C 6 H 5 NH 2 , C 2 H 5 NH 2 , NH 3 Solution: In aqueous solution, basic strength decreases in the order. (C 2 H 5 )NH > (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N > C 2 H 5 NH 2 nh In increasing order of solubility in water: (C 2 H 5 )NH 2 > (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH> C 6 H 5 NH 2 In decreasing order of their pKb values: NH 3 > C 2 H 5 NH 2 > C 6 H 5 NH 2 2 3. Write the Nernst equation for the following cell reaction: Zn(s) + Cu 2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + Cu(s) How will the E cell be affected when concentration of Cu 2+ ions is increased and Zn 2+ ions is increased? Solution: Zn + Cu 2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ + Cu E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} E cell increases when concentration of Cu 2+ ions increases. E cell decreases when concentration of Zn 2+ ions increases. 2 SECTION : B 4. The following ions of the 3d-transition series are given: Ti 4+ , V 3+ , Cr 3+ , Mn 3+ (Atomic number: Ti = 22, V = 23, Cr = 24, Mn = 25) Identify the ion which is most stable in aqueous solution. a strong oxidizing agent. colourless in aqueous solution. Give suitable reason in each. Solution: Ti 4+ = 3d, V 3+ = 3d 3 , Mn 3+ = 3d 4 , Cr 3+ = 3d 3 out of these ions Cr 3+ is most stable due to crystal feild spliting concept. In Cr 3+ , t 2 g in half filled, so extra stable. Mn 3+ is strong oxidising agent because Mn 3+ can easily be converted into Mn 2+ having d 5 configuration which is a stable half-filled configuration. Ti 4+ is colourless because it is having no unpaired e - for excitation to higher energy level and it is colourless. 3 5. (i) On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d 4 ion if {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} . Using valence bond theory, predict the hybridization and magnetic character of [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- . (Atomic number of Ni = 28) Write the formula of the following complex using IUPAC norms: Dichloridobis (ethane-1,2-diamine) cobalt (III) Solution: On the basis of crystal field theory, for a d 4 ion, if {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} < P, then the complex is a high spin complex formed by the association of weak field ligands with the metal ion. As a result, the fourth electron enters one of the e g orbitals, thereby, exhibiting the electronic configuration {tex}t_{2 g}^3 e_g^1{/tex} . Ni atom (z = 28) E.C of Ni. = [Ar]4s 2 3d 8 E.C. of Ni 2+ = [Ar]4s 0 3d 8 It is diamagnetic, due to the absence of unpaired electrons. Shape - square planar. [CoCl 2 (en) 2 ] 3 OR (ii) When a co-ordination compound NiCl 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 6H 2 O mixed with AgNO 3 , 2 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write The structural formula of the complex. Secondary valency of Ni in the complex. IUPAC name of the complex. Solution: [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ]Cl 2 ] Secondary valency = 6 Hexaaquanickel (II) chloride 3 6. A first order reaction is 50% complete in 40 minutes. Calculate the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction. [Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 10 = 1] Solution: {tex}\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{40 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}}{/tex} 90% completion t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} log {tex}\frac {\left[\mathrm{R}_0\right]} {[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303} { 0.693}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 40 {tex}\times{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{0.693}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 132.9 min 3 7. (i) Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis. Carbylamine reaction. Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction. Solution: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: is used for the preparation of aliphatic primary amines. In this reaction, phthalimide is first of all treated with ethanolic KOH to form potassium phthalimide. Potassium phthalimide on treatment with alkyl halide gives N-alkyl phthalimide, which on hydrolysis with dilute hydrochloric acid gives a primary amine as the product. Carbylamine reaction: is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test. {tex}\underset{Primary\;amine}{R-NH_2}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex} {tex}\underset{Carbylamine}{R-NC}{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex} For example, {tex}\underset{Methnamine}{CH_3-NH_2}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex} {tex}\underset{or\;methyl\;isocyanide}{\underset{Methyl\;Carbylamine}{CH_3-NC}}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex} Hoffmann bromamide reaction: When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. This degradation reaction is known as Hoffmann bromamide reaction. This reaction involves the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon atom of the amide to the nitrogen atom. For example, 3 OR (ii) Write the structures of A, B and C in the following reactions: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{Cl}\xrightarrow{KCN}A\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow[{0^\circ\mathrm C}]{{\mathrm{HNO}}_2}\mathrm C{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[\mathrm\Delta]{{\mathrm{NH}}_3}A\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(a\operatorname{) }{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}}\mathrm C{/tex} Solution: 3 8. (i) Account for the following: Transition elements have higher enthalpies of atomisation. Separation of a mixture of Lanthanoid elements is difficult. The {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^0{/tex} value for copper is positive. Solution: Because of greater number of unpaired electrons which cause strong metallic bonding and strong interatomic interaction forces. Because of lanthanoid contraction, similar atomic or ionic radii and similar properties. Because of high {tex}\Delta a H^{\circ}{/tex} and low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} H^{\circ}{/tex} 3 OR (ii) Define transition elements. Which of the d-block elements may not be regarded as the transition elements? Why transition metals generally form coloured compounds? Solution: Transition elements are defined as an element having a d-subshell that is partially filled with electrons, or an element that has the ability to form stable cations with an incompletely filled d-orbital. e.g., Cr has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d 5 4s 1 , partially filled d-subshell, so it is transition element. Zn, Cd and Hg cannot be classified as transition elements as they have completely filled d-subshell, e.g., Zn has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d 10 4 2 , has completely filled d-subshell, so cannot be classified as transition element. Transition elements generally form coloured compounds on account of d-d transitions, due to partial absorption of visible light occur and d-d transition of electron occurs in d-orbital, which causes the colour of the compound. 3 9. An organic compound X with the molecular formula C 5 H 10 O forms 2,4-DNP derivative, does not reduce Tollens' reagent but gives positive iodoform test on heating with I 2 in the presence of NaOH. Compound X gives ethanoic acid and propanoic acid on vigorous oxidation. Write the Structure of the compound X . Structure of the product obtained when compound X reacts with 2,4-DNP reagent. Structures of the products obtained when compound X is heated with I 2 in the presence of NaOH. Solution: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\mathrm O}{\underset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex} CH 3 -CH 2 CH 2 COONa + CHI 3 3 10. The standard Gibbs energy {tex}\left(\Delta \mathrm{rG}^{\circ}\right){/tex} for the following cell reaction is -300 kJ mol -1 : Zn(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} for the reaction. (Given: 1F = 96500 mol -1 ) Calculate {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^o{/tex} for MgCl 2 if {tex}\lambda^o{/tex} values for Mg 2+ ion and Cl - ion are 106 S cm 2 mol -1 and 76.3 S cm 2 mol -1 respectively. Solution: Zn(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) The number of moles of electrons transferred (n) is 2, as two moles of electrons are involved in the reduction of two moles of {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{\wedge}{/tex} + ions. Now, you are given {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -300 {tex}\frac{kJ}{mol}{/tex} , but you need to convert it to joules (J) by multiplying by 1000 (since 1 kJ = 1000 J): {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -300 {tex}\frac{kJ}{mol}{/tex} {tex}\times {/tex} 1000 {tex}\frac{J}{kJ}{/tex} = -300,000 {tex}\frac{J}{mol}{/tex} {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}{/tex} Now, plug in the values into the equation and solve for E o : -300,000 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{mol}}{/tex} = -(2 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{~C}}{\mathrm{mol}}{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} E o Given the cell reaction: Let's calculate E o : E o = {tex}\frac{-300,000 \frac{\mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{mol}}}{-(2 \mathrm{~mol} \times 96500 \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{mol}})}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 3.11 V So, the standard cell potential E o for the given cell reaction is approximately 3.11 V.
